title: Passing the Torch: How Faith Moves Across Generations | Institute for Family Studies
gist: Jesse Smith and Jane Lankes Smith analyze American adults raised Christian to identify what makes faith transmit across generations: parental modeling, daily-life faith practice, regular religion talk, two-parent involvement (especially fathers), marital stability and quality, parent-child relationship quality, media oversight, and embedded church community. They frame faith decline as a slow but compounding intergenerational 'avalanche.'

insights.claim: U.S. religious identification, worship attendance, and belief in God have all dropped by double-digit margins since the 1990s, and the decline is mostly intergenerational — each cohort enters adulthood less faithful — which 'cascades into an avalanche' as devout generations are replaced.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: A compounding generational decline implies the religious-affiliated base shrinks structurally, not cyclically — a long-horizon headwind for faith-linked institutions and markets.
insights.quote: cascades into an avalanche over time as the most devout generations disappear
insights.locator: Religious families in America are in a challenging situation

insights.claim: The decline stems from a 'perfect storm' of factors: a values shift toward individualism, post-Cold War identity change, mid-2000s internet/social-media exposure to atheist arguments, religious moral scandals, culture-war partisanship, delayed family formation, and faith being crowded out by activities and work.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Multi-causal framing means no single intervention reverses the trend — relevant to assessing the feasibility of faith-revival strategies.
insights.quote: the decline of American Christianity is not due to any one thing, but rather multiple forces all working together
insights.locator: There is not any one reason for this decline

insights.claim: The strongest predictor of a child's adult religiosity is parental religiosity during upbringing: 26% of children of weekly-attending parents attend weekly as adults versus 12% otherwise, and 47% of children of daily-praying parents pray daily versus under one-third otherwise.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Quantifies that faith transmission is primarily a home/parent effect, not an institutional one — redirecting where churches should invest.
insights.quote: children tend to mirror what they consistently observe in their parents’ lives
insights.locator: Research confirms that the strongest predictor

insights.claim: Talking about religion at home is one of the strongest predictors of adult faith: children in households where faith was discussed several times a week or more were over twice as likely to attend church, value religion highly, and pray daily as young adults.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
entities: National Study of Youth and Religion
insights.so_what: Counters the conventional fear of 'pushing too hard' — under-discussion, not over-discussion, is the bigger transmission failure.
insights.quote: over twice as likely to attend church, say religion was very important to them, and pray daily in young adulthood
insights.locator: This is clear in the data, which show that talking about religion

insights.claim: Faith formation is disproportionately carried by mothers — just 17% of dads vs 39% of moms claim primary responsibility, and among teens attending with one parent, 79% attend with their mother — yet transmission is strongest when both parents actively promote faith.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Identifies father engagement as the underused lever; the report explicitly recommends pastors 'actively engage fathers.'
insights.quote: just 17% of dads cited themselves, compared to 39% of moms

insights.claim: 41% of children who attend church weekly with both parents go on to attend weekly as adults, dropping to 29% with only one parent — and two-parent faith conversations in one generation predict more such conversations in the next.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Two-parent involvement compounds across generations, quantifying the multi-generational payoff of dual engagement.
insights.quote: two involved parents are better than one
insights.locator: Yet intergenerational transmission of religion is strongest

insights.claim: Children raised by two married parents are most likely to be religious adults; in NSYR data, children from married households have a 19% chance of weekly worship as adults versus 10% from single-parent households.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
entities: National Study of Youth and Religion
insights.so_what: Links marital stability directly to faith retention, tying the report into IFS's broader marriage-revival thesis.
insights.quote: children from married households have a 19% probability of attending weekly worship in adulthood, compared to only 10% for those from single-parent households
insights.locator: When examining the NSYR data set

insights.claim: Marital quality matters beyond stability: 'completely satisfied' parents reported nearly five faith conversations with children per week versus under four for dissatisfied parents, and very-happy marriages predicted a 46% (vs 41%) chance of children praying daily as adults.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Shows happy (not just intact) marriages drive transmission, supporting marriage-enrichment ministry investment.
insights.quote: nearly five faith-related conversations with their children per week, compared to less than four
insights.locator: The importance of happy marriages is affirmed

insights.claim: Boys are far more vulnerable to family disruption for faith retention: in Add Health data, boys in married households have a 14% chance of regular adult worship versus 5% from stepfamilies (a 9-point gap), while the girls' gap (4 points) is not statistically significant.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: A notable sex-differentiated vulnerability — boys' faith retention is disproportionately sensitive to family structure.
insights.quote: boys who grow up in married households have a 14% probability of attending regular worship in adulthood, compared to 5% for boys from stepfamilies
insights.locator: In the Add Health data set

insights.claim: Closer parental monitoring of teens' TV and internet predicted higher adult religiosity across nearly all measures — and since this data was collected in 2003, before smartphones and social media, the authors argue media oversight is almost certainly even more central now.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Connects screen-time governance to faith outcomes, reinforcing the broader IFS family-tech-restriction agenda.
insights.quote: the importance of monitoring electronics in childhood is almost certainly even more central now
insights.locator: It’s worth noting the data on media exposure

insights.claim: Youth programming has lasting impact: 22% of children encouraged to attend youth group attended church weekly at ages 25-28 versus only 9% who were not, and church camps/retreats predicted higher religious commitment a decade later.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: Validates congregation-level youth investment as a measurable retention driver, complementing the home-centered findings.
insights.quote: 22% of children who were encouraged to attend youth group as teenagers attended church weekly at ages 25–28, compared to only 9%
insights.locator: When parents encourage their children to participate in a youth group

insights.claim: The authors propose a 'nested model': the family household is the inner layer (stable, loving relationships plus modeled and discussed faith) and the congregation the outer layer (community, buffer against secularization, youth programming) — children in such a 'nest' are most likely to keep the faith.
insights.speaker: Jesse Smith, Jane Lankes Smith
insights.so_what: A reusable model placing parents at the center and churches as scaffolding — guides how religious institutions should allocate effort.
insights.quote: our analyses support a kind of nested model of effective religious transmission

top_entities: National Study of Youth and Religion
